data.title: Trone runs for old Maryland House seat
data.description:
Former Rep. David Trone (D-Md.), who ran and lost for a Senate seat in Maryland last year, is launching a bid for his old House seat in Maryland’s 6th Congressional District as he looks to make a comeback to Congress next year.

Trone filed paperwork with Federal Election Commission (FEC) on Thursday announcing his intent to run in the district, which includes much of the state’s northern border and cities like Hagerstown and Frederick. 

“We need someone that can really be a fighter,” Trone told The Washington Post in an interview published Thursday. He will be challenging Rep. April McClain Delaney (D-Md.), who he said was “not doing the job” and “not fighting Trump.”

Trone, the co-founder of the alcoholic retailer Total Wine & More, spent tens of millions last cycle in a bid for former Sen. Ben Cardin’s (D-Md.) seat, ultimately losing to Sen. Angela Alsobrooks (D-Md.) in the Democratic primary. 

Trone’s decision to launch a bid for the seat tees up a contentious and likely expensive primary clash against McClain Delaney, who’s serving her first term. 

In a statement on X, the first-term House member suggested she’s not planning to step aside and retire after her current term and pushed back against Trone’s assertation that she wasn’t fighting the president. 

“Now David Trone says I should step aside so he can have his old office back after he ran for the Senate, lost and has been sitting on the sidelines,” McClain Delaney said in an ad responding to Trone’s announcement. “He’s a distraction.” 

2024 Election Coverage

  • 2024 Election News
  • 2024 Election Polling Data
  • 2024 Presidential Election Forecast

The seat went for former Vice President Harris in 2024 by close to 6 points. Though the seat leans Democratic and the party is favored to have a good environment next year, a primary clash adds headaches as they’re looking to take back the House majority next year.

Trone’s announcement comes as a wave of younger Democratic candidates are primarying incumbents, arguing that some in the party have been in Congress too long or are not doing enough to fight against Trump. The former congressman’s entry, albeit is expected to be well-funded, may be running into challenging competing cross-political winds.

One variable that could complicate things further is whether Maryland decides to redistrict. Maryland Gov. Wes Moore (D) appears keen on moving forward with trying to pass an aggressive 8-0 Democratic-favored map, though Maryland Senate President Bill Ferguson (D) has expressed his opposition to the idea.

data.title: La Niña likely for next month, NOAA says. Here’s what it means for winter weather
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – La Niña hasn’t left us just yet. The climate phenomenon is expected to “continue for the next month or two,” national forecasters said in an update Thursday.

This year’s La Niña, which officially arrived on the scene in October, is favored to last through most of the 2025-2026 winter before dissipating sometime early next year, according to the Climate Prediction Center, part of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA).

In the meantime, the climate pattern continues to influence the type of weather we’ll see this winter. Typically, La Niña keeps weather dry and warmer in the southern half of the country, making drought conditions worse in Southern California and the Southwest. On the other hand, the Pacific Northwest and Ohio Valley tend to see more precipitation during a La Niña winter.

That pattern matches up pretty closely with what the Climate Prediction Center is predicting for the next three months: more precipitation – which could be rain or snow – for the northern states, but less-than-average precipitation down south.

La Niña generally divides the country into two fates, but the dividing line moves year to year. That uncertainty shows in the forecast map below, with the middle of the country having equal chances of above-average, below-average and normal precipitation.

The temperature outlook, similarly, divides the country. Northern states are favored to see colder-than-normal weather this winter, according to the long-range forecast. States shaded in orange, especially Arizona, New Mexico and Florida, are favored to see a warmer-than-average winter.

In the nearer term, a dozen states were under a Winter Weather Advisory on Thursday. Heavy snow was falling in parts of the Midwest and Pennsylvania while freezing rain threatened the morning commute in parts of the Plains states. Lake effect snow was also in the forecast for Western New York.

The Pacific Northwest was also being hit by a series of powerful storms. Residents in parts of Oregon and Washington grappled with power outages, flooding and school closures, while drivers navigated debris slides and water that closed roads and submerged vehicles. Forecasters warned that the worst was still to come, with some major rivers expected to crest later in the week.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: Republicans block bill to extend expiring health insurance subsidies
data.description:
Senate Republicans on Thursday blocked a motion to advance a Democrat-drafted bill to extend enhanced health insurance premium subsidies that are due to expire at the end of 2025, tax credits that Democrats say are needed to keep premiums from rising by double-digits next year.

The legislation, which needed 60 votes to advance, failed by a vote of 51 to 48.

Republican Sens. Susan Collins (Maine) and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska) voted for the Democratic plan to extend the enhanced health insurance tax credits, even though both lawmakers have called for reforms to the program. Sen. Dan Sullivan (R-Alaska), who is up for re-election next year, and Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) also voted for the Democratic proposal. 

The unusual vote on a Democratic-crafted health care plan while Republicans control the Senate was set up by a deal Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) struck with centrist Democrats to end a 43-day government shutdown.

The proposal, unveiled by Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.), would have extended the enhanced subsidies, which were first enacted during the COVID-19 pandemic, another three years.

“Our bill is the last train to leave the station. After this, we will not have time to try again before premiums shoot through the roof next year,” Schumer said on the Senate floor before the vote.

Schumer touted the Democratic plan as a “simple extension of these health care tax credits.”

“It’s time for Republicans to join us,” he urged his colleagues.

Earlier in the day, they voted for a Republican plan to convert funding for enhanced insurance premium subsidies into contributions to health savings accounts to pay for out-of-pocket costs. That proposal failed to advance on a mostly party-line vote.

The cost of health plans on the Affordable Care Act are projected to increase by 26 percent on average in 2026, according to an analysis by KFF, a health policy research group.

Enrollees on the ACA’s marketplace who receive enhanced premium subsidies are expected to see their monthly premium payments more than double if those tax credits expire in January.

Schumer signaled that Democrats would campaign on the issue next year ahead of the midterm election. 

“The American people will not forget which way senators vote today,” he warned on the floor. 

Sen. Jeanne Shaheen (D-N.H.), a leading advocate for extending the enhanced subsidies, said millions of Americans will “face a crushing financial blow” when the enhanced subsidies expire.

“These credits have helped millions of families, including tens of thousands of people in New Hampshire, keep their coverage and balance their family budgets,” she said.

Shaheen has participated in bipartisan talks to extend the subsidies, but those negotiations have failed to produce a bipartisan bill that could get the 60 votes needed to advance.

Republicans criticized the Democratic bill for not including any substantial reforms, such as a GOP-backed proposal to prevent households that earn more than $200,000 a year from reaping federal subsidies or another to ensure that lower-income Americans pay something into the system.

“Apparently, they think that a three-year extension with no reforms to try and disguise the real impact of ObamaCare’s spiraling costs is actually a plan. I don’t know how you can call it a plan,” Thune said on the Senate floor.

Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee Chair Bill Cassidy (R-La.) said the enhanced subsidies send too much money to insurance companies and that more of it should go to consumers.

Cassidy teamed up with Senate Finance Committee Chair Mike Crapo (R-Idaho) to introduce a competing GOP proposal to convert the value of the enhanced premium subsidies into payments to health savings accounts for people enrolled through the ACA marketplace to offset their out-of-pocket costs.

“What Democrats want to do, they want to give 100 percent of the money to the insurance companies, and the insurance company, by law under ObamaCare, can take 20 percent of it for overhead and profit. Eighty percent of it goes to patient care,” Cassidy said on the floor before the vote.

Democrats blocked a motion to advance the Cassidy-Crapo health plan in an earlier vote Thursday.

The failure of both bills, means lawmakers likely won’t vote on another proposal to address rising health insurance costs until next year.

Centrist members on both sides of the aisle expressed hope that once the dueling plans failed on the Senate floor, the path might open for more productive bipartisan talks.

“I’m ready to come to the table and I think we would be better served if after we get through these votes we sit down and come to a compromise to address the real costs that people are facing in terms of health care, and I’m ready to do that,” said Shaheen, who spearheaded the deal to reopen the federal government last month.

data.title: Live updates: Senate shoots down dueling health care plans as Obamacare deadline approaches
Watch live: White House holds briefing after US seizes oil tanker near Venezuela
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt will brief reporters Thursday afternoon, a day after President Trump confirmed the U.S. seized an oil tanker near Venezuela.

The move marks the latest escalation between the two countries amid the Trump administration‘s self-declared war on drug cartels and as tensions rise with Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro. It also comes as the Defense Department faces scrutiny over military strikes on alleged drug boats in the Caribbean.

Attorney General Pam Bondi, who shared a video of the seizure, said the U.S. sanctioned the vessel due to its ties to “illicit oil shipping network supporting foreign terrorist organizations.”

The press briefing is scheduled to begin at 1 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Trump administration helped Venezuela’s Machado escape to Norway: Report
data.description:
The Trump administration reportedly aided Nobel Peace Prize winner María Corina Machado in her secret travel from Venezuela to Norway for the award ceremony in Oslo.

Venezuela’s opposition leader has been in hiding since Jan. 9, shortly after President Nicolás Maduro started his third term following a disputed election.

Leaving her location on Monday, she first managed to get through 10 military check points undetected, according to reporting by The Wall Street Journal, citing a person familiar with the operation.

She next headed to Curaçao on Tuesday. Her path through the Caribbean Sea was flanked with danger in waters ripe with attacks from the Trump administration in recent weeks. The Journal reported that the group helping Machado reached out to the U.S. military so it would not strike her boat. In addition to Machado, two people were onboard the fishing skiff that skipped across the sea after a two-month planning process.

“We coordinated that she was going to leave by a specific area so that they would not blow up the boat,” said the person close to the operation, according to the Journal.

“The Trump administration was aware of the operation, said people familiar with the matter, but the extent of its involvement was unclear,” the Journal reported, but noted the U.S. Navy and the Pentagon declined comment and the administration “denied accuracy of the military contact.”

Using flight tracking data, the Journal reported a pair of U.S. Navy F-18s flew into the Gulf of Venezuela and circled in the air for approximately 40 minutes, coinciding with Machado’s travel. 

The White House did not immediately respond to The Hill’s request for comment on the matter.

In Curaçao, she joined a private contractor supplied by the Trump administration who specializes in extractions, the Journal reported, citing a person close to the operation to get her to Oslo.

Machado landed in Oslo on Wednesday, stopping en route in Bangor, Maine. But she did not make it for the Nobel Peace Prize ceremony, with her daughter accepting the award on her behalf.

She appeared early Thursday on a hotel balcony.

“Oslo, here I am!!” Machado wrote in an Instagram caption for her video speaking to a large crowd of supporters from the balcony.

Her arrival comes months after she dedicated her award to President Trump.

“We are on the threshold of victory and today, more than ever, we count on President Trump, the people of the United States, the peoples of Latin America, and the democratic nations of the world as our principal allies to achieve Freedom and democracy,” she wrote  in an October post on the social platform X. 

“I dedicate this prize to the suffering people of Venezuela and to President Trump for his decisive support of our cause!” she continued. 

Venezuela’s leaders say the Trump administration’s strikes on the Latin American country are rooted in oil gains.

“Those extremist, fascist lackeys who have been asking for blockades, invasions and bombings against Venezuela are going to be defeated again, the same way their cheap show in Norway fell apart,” Vice President Delcy Rodriguez said according to the Journal.

Machado is expected to return to Venezuela, “when we believe the security conditions are right, and it won’t depend on whether or not the regime leaves,” the Associated Press reported. She ix expected to make stops in Europe and Washington, the Journal reported.

data.title: Senate GOP health care plan fails on mostly party-line vote
data.description:
A Republican-drafted proposal to set up health savings accounts for people who buy their health insurance on the Affordable Care Act (ACA) marketplace failed to advance on a largely party-line vote on Thursday, leaving Congress no closer to addressing the substantial rise in health care premiums in 2026.

The legislation, which needed 60 votes to advance, failed by a vote of 51 to 48.

Every Democrat voted against it. Sen. Rand Paul (Ky.) was the only Republican to vote “no.”

The plan, drafted by Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee Chair Bill Cassidy (R-La.) and Senate Finance Committee Chair Mike Crapo (R-Idaho), follows President Trump’s exhortations to redirect federal subsidies that have gone to insurance companies to instead go directly to people.

The Cassidy-Crapo plan would convert the value of funding for enhanced premium tax credits under the ACA into federal contributions to health savings accounts (HSAs) to defray out-of-pocket health care costs, according to an analysis by KFF, a health policy research group.  

The plan would incentivize people to purchase high-deductible plans with lower monthly premiums and rely on HSAs to reduce their personal costs. It would also expand the availability of catastrophic plans available on the federal marketplace.

The Cassidy-Crapo proposal was one of two competing health care plans up for a vote on Thursday as Congress scrambles to address the looming spike in health care costs. The Senate is also set to vote on a Democratic proposal to extend the enhanced ACA subsidies for three years.

Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) slammed the GOP proposal as “ridiculous” shortly before the vote.

He argued it falls short of what’s needed to keep health insurance premiums from rising dramatically because “it doesn’t extend the ACA tax credits for a day, a month, a year, none of that, not even a day.”

“The Republican bill is little more than junk insurance,” he said on the floor.

Cassidy and Crapo, however, argue that giving federal funding to patients instead of insurance companies will give them more control over their health care coverage.

Speaking on the Senate floor, Cassidy said that under his plan, “everybody up to age 49 gets a thousand dollars into their health savings account” and a prospective family of four would get $5,000.

The Louisiana senator argued that his plan would “empower the patient to shop, driving competition, lowering health care costs.”

Crapo emphasized the GOP plan hews to Trump’s directive to send money to American families instead of companies.

“Patients should decide where their health care spending goes, not insurance companies. In line with President Trump’s call to direct money to patients instead of insurance companies, this approach builds on the success of the Working Families Tax Cuts Act, which expanded health savings accounts and their eligibility to be paired with more affordable insurance plans,” Crapo said on the floor, referring to language promoting health savings accounts in Trump’s Big, Beautiful Act, which was signed into law in July.

The HSAs under the Cassidy-Crapo plan would be available to people who buy their insurance on the ObamaCare marketplace, unlike a competing proposal from Sen. Rick Scott (R-Fla.) that would create Trump Health Freedom Accounts that would be more widely available.

Some Republicans have criticized the Cassidy-Crapo plan for working within the parameters of the ACA, which theyattempted to repeal in Trump’s first term in 2017.

Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) argued in a post on X, the social media platform, that funneling ACA subsidies into HSAs instead of to insurance companies “may be a slight improvement.”

“But it’s still a vote for ObamaCare — something conservatives would have firmly rejected not long ago,” he said.

Other Republicans, such as Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska), expressed concern that the Cassidy-Crapo plan did not include any extension of the enhanced health insurance premium subsidies that are due to expire in January.

Sens. Bernie Moreno (R-Ohio) and Susan Collins (R-Maine) unveiled a proposal earlier this week to extend the expiring subsidies for two years and implement several reforms, such as limiting their availability to families earning less than $200,000 in annual income. 

Murkowski, Moreno and Collins all voted for the Cassidy-Crapo proposal.

data.title: Goldman on Lander challenge: ‘Politics will have to wait ’til 2026′
data.description:
Rep. Dan Goldman (D-N.Y.) on Wednesday declined to comment on New York City Comptroller Brad Lander’s (D) decision to challenge him in the party primary, instead homing in on legislative tasks to complete before the year ends.

“I’m not really thinking about this right now. I’m in Washington, really focused on making sure that health care will not be taken away from millions of people,” Goldman said during a Wednesday appearance on PIX’s “Politics Daily.”

“We are focused on the immigration dragnet that Donald Trump is sweeping the country with and that is disappearing people who are lawfully applying for immigration status in this country, taking them out of courthouses, taking them off the streets, away from their families,” he continued.

Goldman added, “So I’m really laser focused on the work we’re doing right now. And politics will have to wait ’til 2026.”

Lander launched his congressional campaign on Wednesday, hitting Goldman for being an “adversary” rather than an “ally” to New York Mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani, who gained a large following for his plans to implement citywide policies to promote affordability.

The comptroller suspended his mayoral campaign after losing the primary earlier this year and officially endorsed Mamdani as the party nominee, while Goldman, Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) and others declined to do the same.

“I did not endorse anyone. I stayed out of the race, but it was never about his affordability agenda, which I support and have always supported. I worked with Zohran actually on a pilot program for the free bus fares,” Goldman, one of the richest members of Congress, told host Dan Mannarino.

“I, as I mentioned earlier, have been a huge supporter of universal childcare long before this mayoral race and strongly believe that that is a necessary future. I have been working on the front lines here in Washington to address that affordability agenda. And I have spoken with Zohran several times since the election, offered to work with him, eager to work with him,” the Levi-Strauss heir stated.

Lander has already been endorsed by Mamdani and Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) ahead of next year’s primary. 

However, he’ll need to build a large war chest to rival Goldman who’s valued at $253 million, according to Bloomberg.

data.title: Education Department announces $208M in mental health grants after previous cancelations
data.description:
The Education Department announced on Thursday it is handing out $208 million in grants to the Mental Health Service Professional Demonstration and School-Based Mental Health programs. 

The announcement comes after the department canceled over 200 school-based mental health grants from the Biden administration era. 

The Trump administration argued those grants “placed ideology above serving students” and that it “prioritized the racial characteristics of providers and divisive ideologies” over mental health interventions. 

“Under the Biden Administration, it was more important to shape the racial and gender identities of mental health providers than it was to focus resources on high-quality, credentialed school psychologists who are best positioned to serve American students when they are at their most vulnerable,” said Secretary of Education Linda McMahon. 

“While there was doubt that the Trump Administration could right this wrong and re-compete these dollars before the end of the year, we are proud to announce that we did exactly that. Today, over $200 million is being awarded directly to states and school districts to support student mental health — a meaningful win for our education system,” she added. 

The new awards with go to 65 grant recipients that the Trump administration is encouraging to focus on “merit-based personnel hiring and evidence-based student services.” 

The purpose of the grants is to hire more school psychologists and to build resources so the mental health resources can continue after the grant. 

Around half of the grant recipients will go to rural communities, giving $120 million to those communities.

data.title: Warren: Hassett ‘biggest concern’ among Fed chair contenders
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) said Thursday that of the top contenders to replace Jerome Powell as Federal Reserve chair, she is most concerned with National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett.

“I am most worried about Kevin Hassett. … He has made clear that he will do whatever the president wants done,” Warren said on CNBC. “He will say whatever the president wants said.”

The ranking member on the Senate Banking Committee also called Hassett the “very definition of a sock puppet.”

The Fed cut interest rates by a quarter point for the third straight meeting on Wednesday, lowering the federal funds rate range to 3.5 and 3.75 percent. The decision from the Federal Open Market Committee was likely its last before President Trump announces Powell’s successor.

The president, who nominated Powell to lead the central bank during his first term, has frequently criticized the Fed chair for not backing an earlier or larger rate cut. 

Warren has also criticized Powell in the past for holding rates steady. She argued Thursday, though, that the Fed did not cut rates earlier this year due to “chaos” resulting from Trump’s tariffs.

After the Fed announced Wednesday’s rate cut, Powell said that the rise in inflation, likely exacerbated by the levies, could be a one-time increase if the president does not hike the import taxes significantly.

“What the president’s been doing on tariffs is not just a tax in the sense of driving prices up, it’s a tax in the sense of keeping interest rates higher than they otherwise would be,” Warren added.

Powell’s term expires in May, with Hassett, former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh and sitting Governor Christopher Waller among the candidates to replace him. Before taking office, the president’s nominee will need to be approved by the Banking Committee and full Senate.

Warren said that Trump is “looking for someone who will do his bidding” as Fed chair, and expressed hope that her Republican colleagues will value the independence of the Fed.

“I think this is going to be one more moment: Are Republicans really willing to stand up for the principles they say they believe in, like an independent Fed, or are they just going to say [that] whatever Donald Trump wants is what they’ll go along with,” the Massachusetts Democrat noted.
